evidence_quotes.quote: I am a person that buys and plays a whole lot of video games, mainly on the PC.
I have always been very wary of buying always-online games or any games that depend on a server to function properly.
Every game that depends on an external server (requires internet access) effectively has planned obsolesence.

paragraphs.text: (I'll make the comment in english so that we don't have to deal with translations, I'm a Swede to be clear) Consumer protection is extremely important and I fully welcome every initiative to strengthen it. Nothing infuriates me more than small powerless individuals getting trod upon by big powerful corporations (or other entities) that effectively are above the law due to their vast amount of resources. Thankfully the EU seems to be on the same page, generally, and it is very gratifying to see that we have a much stronger consumer protection in the EU than what they have in many places outside of the EU. Now to the topic at hand and why I am writing this comment. I am a person that buys and plays a whole lot of video games, mainly on the PC. I have always been very wary of buying always-online games or any games that depend on a server to function properly. I have never understood why this is legal in the way that it to design games in this fashion since it completely removes every trace of consumer protection. You buy a product, in this case a video-game, and for all you know the game can be non-functional within a week, all based on the whims and fortunes of the company that made the game. And most games aren't cheap either, new games often go for between 50 and 100 euros, depending on the game. Imagine if this was the case for any other product. You could get a refund in most of those cases, but for games? Not a chance, for some reason. There's no scarcity, since the games are digital, and they don't depend on hardware components that naturally degrade. Every game that depends on an external server (requires internet access) effectively has planned obsolesence. The games WILL cease to function at some point in the future, and it's very avoidable. I'm a programmer by trade myself, it would be trivial to design any game in such a way that this whole situation was avoided. Long story short, please consider adding this problem to the initiative/act as well. Planned obsolescence should never be allowed, hardware or software.
